1: εἰρήνην ἔχομεν πρὸς τὸν θεόν (eirēnēn echomen pros ton theon) — "we have peace with God." The indicative echomen (we have) versus the subjunctive schōmen (let us have) is one of the most debated textual variants in the NT. External evidence slightly favors the subjunctive (Codices Sinaiticus, Vaticanus, Alexandrinus), but the indicative makes better logical sense in context: Paul has just argued for justification by faith and now states its consequences. Eirēnē here is the OT shalom — not merely absence of conflict but positive relational wholeness with God.
2:
τὴν προσαγωγὴν ἐσχήκαμεν (tēn prosagōgēn eschēkamen) — "we have gained access" — perfect active of echō: a past gaining with continuing possession. Prosagōgē (access, approach) is a technical term from the royal court: the formal introduction that grants audience with the king; or from the temple: the approach to the divine presence. Through Christ the believer has been formally introduced into the divine presence and maintains ongoing access.

ἐν ᾗ ἑστήκαμεν (en hē hestēkamen) — "in which we stand" — perfect active of histēmi: having taken a position and remaining in it. Grace is not merely the door through which one passes but the sphere in which one permanently stands.
3: καυχώμεθα ἐν ταῖς θλίψεσιν (kauchōmetha en tais thlipsesin) — "we boast in our sufferings" — the same verb (kauchaomai) used for the boasting that faith excludes (Romans 3:27); now Paul redirects it toward sufferings. Thlipsis (pressure, affliction, tribulation) — from thlibō (to press, squeeze); concrete physical-social suffering, not merely inconvenience. εἰδότες ὅτι (eidotes hoti) — "knowing that" — the boasting is knowledge-based, not masochistic; the chain that follows (suffering → endurance → character → hope) is the rationale.
4: ἡ δὲ ὑπομονὴ δοκιμήν (hē de hypomonē dokimēn) — "and endurance produces character" — hypomonē (endurance, remaining-under) and dokimē (tested character, that which has passed the test). The metallurgical testing image: the pressured metal that passes the test becomes certified as genuine. Suffering → tested endurance → certified character is the logic.
5: οὐ καταισχύνει (ou kataischynei) — "does not put to shame/disappoint" — present active: the hope is continuously non-disappointing. The Roman context makes this pointed: hope in crucified criminals typically did put one to shame. Paul's claim is that this particular hope is vindicated by the Spirit's testimony. ἐκκέχυται (ekkechytai) — "has been poured out" — perfect passive: the divine love was poured and remains poured, a completed and abiding reality rather than a past event now concluded.
6: ἔτι γὰρ Χριστὸς ὄντων ἡμῶν ἀσθενῶν κατὰ καιρὸν ὑπὲρ ἀσεβῶν ἀπέθανεν — "for while we were still weak, at the right time Christ died for the ungodly." Kata kairon (at the right moment, the appointed time) — kairos is qualitative time (significant moment) rather than chronological time (chronos); the timing of Christ's death is providentially exact. Asebōn (for the ungodly) — the same word as Romans 4:5; Christ died for exactly those whom God justifies.
7: μόλις γὰρ ὑπὲρ δικαίου τις ἀποθανεῖται — "for one would scarcely die for a righteous person" — the moral gradient Paul establishes: hardly anyone dies for the righteous (dikaios); perhaps someone dares to die for the genuinely good (agathos). The distinction between the merely-law-keeping righteous and the lovable-good represents the upper limit of human self-sacrifice.
8: συνίστησιν δὲ τὴν ἑαυτοῦ ἀγάπην εἰς ἡμᾶς ὁ θεός (synistēsin de tēn heautou agapēn eis hēmas ho theos) — "but God demonstrates his own love toward us." Synistēmi (to establish, demonstrate, commend, prove) — more than display; the cross is a proof, an argument, a demonstration of the character of divine love. The emphasis on "his own" (heautou) and "toward us" (eis hēmas) makes the love personal and directed.
9: πολλῷ οὖν μᾶλλον (pollō oun mallon) — "much more therefore" — the a-fortiori argument Paul will use repeatedly in vv9-11: if X (the greater) has been accomplished, how much more certainly Y (the lesser) will follow. Justification by Christ's blood is the greater (costly, accomplished while we were sinners); salvation from wrath is the lesser (what follows from the accomplished justification).
10: εἰ γὰρ ἐχθροὶ ὄντες κατηλλάγημεν τῷ θεῷ (ei gar echthroi ontes katēllagēmen tō theō) — "for if while being enemies we were reconciled to God." Echthroi ontes — present participle: enemies as a present active state. Katēllagēmen — aorist passive: the reconciliation was accomplished from God's side, not worked up from ours. The a-fortiori: if reconciliation happened while we were enemies, how much more will salvation be certain now that we are friends. διὰ τοῦ θανάτου τοῦ υἱοῦ αὐτοῦ — "through the death of his Son" — reconciliation grounds in the most extreme possible divine act.
11: καυχώμενοι ἐν τῷ θεῷ (kauchōmenoi en tō theō) — "boasting in God" — the third boasting in the chapter (v2: in hope; v3: in sufferings; now in God himself). The movement is: from circumstances → to suffering → to the God who is the ground of all three. Katallagē (reconciliation) — from allassō (to exchange); reconciliation as exchange: God takes our enmity and gives his friendship. The exchange theology Paul is building toward (Romans 5:12-21) begins here.
12: δι᾽ ἑνὸς ἀνθρώπου ἡ ἁμαρτία εἰς τὸν κόσμον εἰσῆλθεν — "through one man sin entered the world." The historical singular act: Adam's disobedience as the point of entry. ἐφ᾽ ᾧ πάντες ἥμαρτον (eph' hō pantes hēmarton) — the most debated phrase in the passage. Eph' hō can mean (a) "because of whom" (relative pronoun — in whom = in Adam all sinned, Augustinian reading), (b) "in that" or "because" (conjunction — because all individually sinned, Eastern reading). The causal reading is grammatically more natural in Koine Greek; Paul probably means "because all individually sinned" while also holding the Adamic solidarity of vv15-21.
13: ἄχρι γὰρ νόμου ἁμαρτία ἦν ἐν κόσμῳ (achri gar nomou hamartia ēn en kosmō) — "for until the law sin was in the world." Paul distinguishes sin (hamartia, the reality) from transgression (parabasis, knowing violation of a known command): sin existed between Adam and Moses but could not be formally charged as parabasis without a codified law.
14: τύπος τοῦ μέλλοντος (typos tou mellontos) — "a type/pattern of the one to come." Typos (stamp, impression, figure) — the word from which "typology" derives; Adam is the structural pattern whose negative is filled positively by Christ. The type/antitype relationship is explicit here: Adam's one-act-of-disobedience-with-universal-consequence finds its counter in Christ's one-act-of-obedience-with-universal-consequence (v18-19).
15: οὐχ ὡς τὸ παράπτωμα, οὕτως καὶ τὸ χάρισμα (ouch hōs to paraptōma, houtōs kai to charisma) — "but the gift is not like the trespass." Paraptōma (a falling-beside, trespass, deviation) and charisma (grace-gift). The dissimilarity Paul will emphasize: the trespass and the gift operate by different logic — one produces death through one; the other overflows life through many. The pollō mallon (much more) again: if the trespass accomplished much damage, the grace accomplishes overwhelmingly more.
16: κρίμα μὲν γὰρ ἐξ ἑνὸς εἰς κατάκριμα, τὸ δὲ χάρισμα ἐκ πολλῶν παραπτωμάτων εἰς δικαίωμα — "judgment from one sin brought condemnation, but the gift from many trespasses brought justification." The asymmetry: the condemnation came from one trespass; the justification covers many trespasses. Katakrima (condemnation, the sentence itself) versus dikaiōma (justification, righteous decree/act). Grace absorbs what law multiplies.
17: ἐν ζωῇ βασιλεύσουσιν (en zōē basileusousin) — "they will reign in life" — the eschatological reversal of Adam's condition: not merely restored to pre-fall status but elevated to reigning. The future tense (basileusousin) is both already-realized (in the Spirit's firstfruits) and still-anticipated (at the resurrection). Those who receive the abundance of grace become kings where Adam became a slave to death.
18: δι᾽ ἑνὸς δικαιώματος (di' henos dikaiōmatos) — "through one righteous act" — dikaiōma here means the single act of obedience (Christ's death as fulfillment of righteousness) rather than the legal verdict. The parallel with v16's "one trespass" is exact. εἰς δικαίωσιν ζωῆς — "resulting in justification of life" — the genitive zōēs (life) describes the character of the justification: it is a justification that produces life rather than merely legal standing.
19: διὰ τῆς ὑπακοῆς τοῦ ἑνός (dia tēs hypakoēs tou henos) — "through the obedience of the one." The contrast with "the one man's disobedience" is exact. Hypakoē (obedience, literally: hearing-under) — the obedient life of Christ including but not limited to his death. κατασταθήσονται δίκαιοι (katastathēsontai dikaioi) — "will be constituted righteous" — future passive; the eschatological establishment of righteousness in the many, not merely its forensic declaration.
20: νόμος δὲ παρεισῆλθεν (nomos de pareisēlthen) — "the law came in alongside" — pareiserchomai (to come in beside, slip in alongside) — the verb suggests the law's role as secondary, not primary: it entered the scene that had already been established by Adam's sin and the promise to Abraham. ἵνα πλεονάσῃ τὸ παράπτωμα — "so that the trespass might increase" — the purpose clause is deliberately provocative: the law's purpose includes the multiplication of trespass (clarifying, specifying, and thus increasing the catalog of violations). But: οὗ δὲ ἐπλεόνασεν ἡ ἁμαρτία, ὑπερεπερίσσευσεν ἡ χάρις — "where sin increased, grace superabounded." Hyperperisseuō (to overflow beyond all measure) — the hyper- prefix intensifies the already-intense perisseuō; grace exceeds sin by a qualitative order of magnitude, not merely a quantitative surplus.
21: βασιλεύσῃ ἡ χάρις διὰ δικαιοσύνης εἰς ζωὴν αἰώνιον (basileusē hē charis dia dikaiosynēs eis zōēn aiōnion) — "grace might reign through righteousness unto eternal life." Grace is personified as the royal power that replaces sin's reign (v21a); its instrument is righteousness and its destination is eternal life. The political metaphor (reign, kingship) that has governed vv12-21 reaches its conclusion: the governance of reality has changed sovereigns through Jesus Christ.
